subject: [PATCH] btrfs: replace writeback inhibition xarray with a fixed inline buffer

reviews.author: Sun YangKai
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Sun YangKai noted that the design is clean but suggested non-blocking improvements

Reviewer Sun YangKai suggested using an overflowing add operation with masking to replace the modulo operator when indexing into the fixed inline buffer, and questioned the usefulness of storing additional information in the higher bits.

The reviewer suggested extracting the inhibit preparation logic into a helper function, likely to improve code organization and readability.

Reviewer Sun YangKai noted that using u8 for inhibited_ebs fields is a straightforward choice, even if the limit is later extended to 16 slots, and only the bitmap field would need to be widened to u16.
sentiment_signals: no clear disagreement or request for change
reviews.raw_body:
Reviewed-by: Sun YangKai <sunk67188@gmail.com>

Thanks for your great work. The overall direction looks nice and the 
design is clean. I've got some non\u2011blocking suggestions, though.

---

I think we can using overflowing add with ` & (SLOTS - 1)` as the index 
as long as SLOTS is power of 2, instead of `% SLOTS`. And the higher 
bits can parsed as number of "rounds" if we keep them, and I'm not sure 
if the extra info is useful for any purpose.

---

And personally I'd love to wrap this part of logic into a helper 
function like

static inline u8 btrfs_inhibit_prepare_slot(struct btrfs_trans_handle 
*trans) {...}

Or something with a better name.

---

Given the 8-slot limit for inhibited_ebs (4 bits (8) for capacity, 8 
bits for bitmap), using u8 for these two fields is a clear and 
straightforward choice IMO. Even if we later extend the limit to 16, 
only the bitmap field (inhibited_ebs_referenced) would need to be 
widened to u16.

Thanks,
Sun YangKai

2026-06-25.patch_summary: This patch replaces the xarray used to track inhibited extent buffers in Btrfs transactions with a fixed inline buffer, managed by a CLOCK eviction policy. This change addresses two issues: a write_iops regression caused by frequent xarray stores during COW operations and an unbounded cleanup walk due to long iteration over inhibited buffers. The new approach uses a small fixed-size array to track the working set of buffers revisited across search_again descents, with a CLOCK eviction policy that ensures frequently accessed buffers are retained while less frequently accessed ones are reclaimed first.

2026-06-24.patch_summary: This patch replaces the xarray used to track inhibited extent buffers in Btrfs transactions with a fixed inline buffer, managed by a CLOCK eviction policy. This change addresses two issues: a write_iops regression caused by frequent xarray stores during COW operations and an unbounded cleanup walk due to long iteration over inhibited buffers. The new approach uses a small fixed-size array to track the working set of buffers revisited across search_again descents, with a CLOCK eviction policy that ensures frequently accessed buffers are retained while less frequently accessed ones are reclaimed first.

reviews.author: David Sterba
reviews.summary:
Reviewer David Sterba suggested checking the compiler's optimization of immediate constants, specifically looking for strength reduction from division to bitwise operations.

Reviewer David Sterba noted that the fixed inline buffer size could be reduced from u32 to u8, potentially saving space if transaction handle sizes increase beyond what fits on common slabs.
reviews.raw_body:
For immediate constants the compiler does various transformations that
utilize the power of two abilities to reduce the operations (I think
it's called 'strength reduce', e.g. mod -> bitwise and). You can check
the assembly if there's a division or and.

---

The u32 is a bit more convenient on the instruction level, we can
consider u8 in case the transaction handle size grows more than what
fits to common slabs.

The xarray version has 184 bytes and quick estimate of this patch is
about 240 so we're in the 256 bucket anyway.

reviews.author: David Sterba
reviews.summary:
Reviewer David Sterba noted that the patch's performance improvement is significant (22% drop), but suggested pushing it to a later release cycle (7.2) and requested additional testing before inclusion in the kernel.

Reviewer David Sterba requested that the patch description be reformatted for better readability, specifically suggesting a table or splitting the paragraph by case, and also pointing out that the numbers in the text are hard to see.

Reviewer David Sterba suggested adding a comment to acknowledge the issue and its impact, but did not provide specific technical details about the fix.

Reviewer David Sterba pointed out that using an 'int' instead of a larger type for simple indexes does not affect bit shifting operations, suggesting this could simplify the code.

Reviewer David Sterba suggested using static_assert to enforce constraints on the size of the fixed inline buffer, specifically providing a power-of-two check using the expression 'n != 0 && (n & (n - 1)) == 0'.

Reviewer David Sterba pointed out that due to alignment constraints, a u32 can be used instead of a narrower type, and noted that most of the tracking is still done in the inhibited_ebs array.
reviews.raw_body:
22% drop is quite significant, it's now in 7.1. It could be possible to
push that to 7.2 as it's fixing only the tracking mechanism. We'll need
to get it tested for some time, so estimated rc would be like rc3 or rc4
at most.

---

Can you please transform the paragraph to a table or split it by case?
The numbers are burried in text, hard to see what's the actual benefit.

---

Would be good to comment under the report that you're aware of the
issue, not necessarily say how you're fixing it or when.

---

For simple indexes we use 'int', it does not make any differences for
the bit shifts when it's the number.

---

Please use static_assert for the constraints, for power of two there's
the "n != 0 && (n & (n - 1)) == 0" trick that also works for the
assertion.

---

Due to alignment constraints you can use u32 too, thre will be a hole
after it anyway.

As mentioned in my other reply, the structure size is under 256 so we
don't have to save space with narrow types, majority of the tracking is
still in the inhibited_ebs array.

2026-06-30.patch_summary: This patch replaces the xarray used to track inhibited extent buffers in Btrfs transactions with a fixed inline buffer, managed by a CLOCK eviction policy. This change addresses two issues: a write_iops regression caused by frequent xarray stores during COW operations and an unbounded cleanup walk due to long iteration over inhibited buffers. The new approach uses a small fixed-size array to track the working set of buffers revisited across search_again descents, with a CLOCK eviction policy that ensures frequently accessed buffers are retained while less frequently accessed ones are reclaimed first.
